title: Testing physics beyond the standard model through additional clock transitions in neutral ytterbium
summary: The paper analyzes additional Yb clock transitions including the inner-shell J=2 state and quantifies lifetimes, shifts, and sensitivity coefficients for beyond-Standard-Model tests; this directly motivates using the J=2 manifold as a long-lived Raman intermediate.
key_equations: (H_HF - epsilon_c) delta psi_c = -(F_hat + delta V^F) psi_c
W = -(1/6) sum_{ij} Q_{ij} (partial E_j / partial x_i)|_{x=0}
assumptions: CIPT+RPA atomic-structure treatment
Clock performance inferred from calculated matrix elements and shifts
contributions: Computed properties of 1S0-3P2 and 1S0-4f13 5d6s2(J=2) candidate clock transitions
Provided sensitivity estimates to alpha-variation and gravitational potential
claims: Both additional transitions can support high-accuracy clock operation
The J=2 branch is promising for new-physics sensitivity
conclusions: Neutral Yb supports a multi-clock-transition strategy with distinct sensitivities
limitations: Results are theory-led and depend on atomic-structure accuracy
No direct gate-speed benchmarking
future_work: Experimental realization and precision spectroscopy of the J=2 transition
evidence: Full-text PDF extraction from local seed resource

title: Observation of an Inner-Shell Orbital Clock Transition in Neutral Ytterbium Atoms
summary: First observation and characterization of the 1S0 to inner-shell J=2 transition in neutral Yb, including hyperfine/Zeeman resolution, magic wavelengths, and trap lifetime, providing core experimental parameters for Raman-gate feasibility estimates.
key_equations: Measured trap lifetime in 3D lattice: tau = 1.9(1) s
assumptions: Spectroscopic linewidth and loss interpreted under MOT/lattice conditions
Magic-wavelength extraction assumes calibrated polarization geometry
contributions: Direct spectroscopy for all stable isotopes
Magic-wavelength and lifetime characterization relevant to clock/gate engineering
claims: Inner-shell transition is experimentally accessible and metrologically useful
conclusions: The J=2 transition is a viable platform for improved clock stability and new-physics searches
limitations: Initial demonstration regime; not yet a full operational clock
Observed loss channels in excited manifold
future_work: Higher-precision spectroscopy and clock operation on this transition
